pkg/hasher.purpose: Minimal hashing utility: defines Hash (uint64 type alias) and Hasher interface, backed by a single xxhash implementation. Used for fast, non-cryptographic content hashing — primarily lock key derivation in pkg/framework/lockr.

patterns.name: Hash = uint64 type alias
patterns.description: Hash is a type alias (= uint64), not a named type — interchangeable with uint64 at call sites. Consumers needing nominal typing must define their own wrapper.

patterns.name: Hasher interface for injectable hashing
patterns.description: Structs that hash themselves implement Hasher by returning a Hash from Hash(). Inject via the interface, not via direct xxhash calls.
patterns.example: func (k myKey) Hash() hasher.Hash { return hasher.NewHash([]byte(k.id)) }

key_file_guides.role: Defines Hash type alias and Hasher interface. Source of truth for the contract.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Changing Hash from a type alias to a named type would be a breaking change for all callers assigning uint64 literals.

key_file_guides.role: NewHash([]byte) Hash — the only concrete hash function, using cespare/xxhash v2.
key_file_guides.watch_for: xxhash is non-cryptographic — never use for security-sensitive hashing (passwords, signatures); use crypto/sha256 instead.

anti_patterns: Using hasher.NewHash for cryptographic purposes (authentication, signatures) — use crypto/sha256 or similar
Importing cespare/xxhash directly in domain packages instead of going through hasher.NewHash
Treating Hash as a named type in type assertions — it is a type alias for uint64

decisions.decision: Hash is a type alias rather than a named type
decisions.rationale: Allows callers (particularly lockr) to pass the result directly as uint64 to pg_advisory_xact_lock without an explicit conversion, reducing boilerplate.

pkg/idempotency.purpose: Single-purpose package that generates UUIDv7 idempotency keys for external API calls (e.g., Stripe). UUIDv7 provides monotonic, time-ordered keys safe to use as idempotency tokens without a shared counter.

patterns.name: Key() in production, MustKey() in tests/init only
patterns.description: Key() returns (string, error) and must be used in all application paths. MustKey() panics on failure and is only appropriate in test setup or package-level init.
patterns.example: key, err := idempotency.Key(); if err != nil { return fmt.Errorf("idempotency key: %w", err) }

patterns.name: One key per request — never reuse
patterns.description: Each external API call requiring idempotency must call Key() for a fresh UUIDv7. Reusing a key causes the remote API to deduplicate distinct operations.
patterns.example: key, err := idempotency.Key() // pass key to stripe.ChargeCreate or similar

key_file_guides.role: Entire package — exports Key() and MustKey(). uuid.NewV7() is the sole dependency.
key_file_guides.watch_for: uuid.NewV7() can fail with broken entropy sources — always handle the error in production. MustKey() panics; never call it inside request handlers.

anti_patterns: Using MustKey() in request-handling code paths — prefer Key() and propagate errors
Reusing the same key across multiple requests — each call to Key() must produce a fresh key
Hand-rolling UUID generation instead of using this package — breaks the centralized key strategy

decisions.decision: UUIDv7 instead of UUIDv4 for idempotency keys
decisions.rationale: UUIDv7 is time-ordered, making keys sortable and cache-friendly in database indexes while still globally unique — important for Stripe and similar APIs storing idempotency keys in indexed tables.

code_examples.scenario: Generate a fresh idempotency key before a Stripe charge call

pkg/log.purpose: Provides two orthogonal logging utilities: a testify-based mock for slog.Handler used in unit tests, and a PanicLogger recover-and-log helper for goroutine entry points. This is infrastructure glue, not a logger factory — callers must use slog.Default() for actual logging.

patterns.name: PanicLogger via defer with explicit propagation strategy
patterns.description: Always invoke PanicLogger as a deferred call at the top of a goroutine or main, passing exactly one propagation option. The zero value (no option) re-panics — pass WithExit or WithContinue explicitly for servers and workers.
patterns.example:
defer log.PanicLogger(log.WithExit)   // server main
defer log.PanicLogger(log.WithContinue) // worker loop iteration

patterns.name: OTelCodeStackTrace constant for stack trace attribute key
patterns.description: Stack traces must be logged under the OTelCodeStackTrace constant ("code.stacktrace") per OpenTelemetry semantic conventions. Never use a raw string.

patterns.name: Compile-time slog.Handler interface assertion for MockHandler
patterns.description: var _ slog.Handler = &MockHandler{} enforces interface compliance at compile time; any new mock must include the same assertion.

key_file_guides.role: Recover-and-log wrapper; exposes PanicLogger and three propagation strategies (WithRePanic, WithExit, WithContinue).
key_file_guides.watch_for: Default strategy (zero value) is RePanic — omitting any option re-panics, which is rarely correct for worker goroutines. Always pass WithExit or WithContinue explicitly.

key_file_guides.role: testify mock for slog.Handler; use only in tests.
key_file_guides.watch_for: MockHandler.WithAttrs and WithGroup return values via type assertion on mock.Called — if the expectation is not set up, the assertion panics at test runtime.

anti_patterns: Adding a custom logger constructor here — callers should use slog.Default() or inject slog.Logger externally
Using a raw string instead of OTelCodeStackTrace for stack trace attribute keys
Invoking PanicLogger without defer — it must run inside a recover() scope
Calling PanicLogger with no options in a worker loop — the default re-panic strategy terminates the worker

decisions.decision: Three named propagation strategies instead of a boolean re-panic flag
decisions.rationale: Workers (WithContinue), long-running servers (WithExit), and test helpers (WithRePanic) have fundamentally different needs; a single boolean would conflate exit-process vs continue-loop semantics.

pkg/lrux.purpose: Generic LRU cache with per-item TTL and a synchronous Fetcher callback, wrapping hashicorp/golang-lru/v2. All time comparisons use pkg/clock (not time.Now) so tests can freeze time deterministically.

patterns.name: Use clock.Now() never time.Now()
patterns.description: All TTL comparisons use clock.Now() so tests can freeze time with clock.FreezeTime / clock.SetTime. Any new code here must follow this.

patterns.name: Functional options via cacheOptionsFunc
patterns.description: Constructor accepts variadic cacheOptionsFunc. New options must define a func(*cacheOptions) and expose a With* factory like WithTTL.
patterns.example: func WithTTL(ttl time.Duration) cacheOptionsFunc { return func(o *cacheOptions) { o.ttl = ttl } }

patterns.name: Zero TTL means never-expire via zero time.Time
patterns.description: When ttl == 0, ExpiresAt is the zero time.Time{}. item.ExpiresAt.IsZero() short-circuits to cache-hit, so ttl=0 is a never-expire signal, not zero-second expiry.
patterns.example: // ttl=0 => ExpiresAt stays zero => item never expires

patterns.name: Fetcher must not be nil — validate at construction
patterns.description: Constructor returns an error if fetcher is nil; the package does no lazy validation.
patterns.example: if fetcher == nil { return nil, fmt.Errorf("fetcher is required") }

patterns.name: t.Context() in tests, defer clock.UnFreeze()
patterns.description: All tests use t.Context() and defer clock.UnFreeze() immediately after clock.FreezeTime to prevent time leakage across parallel tests.
patterns.example: clock.FreezeTime(baseTime); defer clock.UnFreeze(); item, err := cache.Get(t.Context(), "key")

key_file_guides.role: Entire package — CacheWithItemTTL struct, Fetcher type, NewCacheWithItemTTL, Get, Refresh, fetchItem.
key_file_guides.watch_for: No mutex around fetch+store: concurrent Gets on an expired key issue multiple simultaneous fetcher calls. Intentional for simplicity but consider when the fetcher is expensive or has side effects.

key_file_guides.role: Unit tests validating TTL boundary behavior, cache hit/miss transitions, and error propagation from the fetcher.
key_file_guides.watch_for: Tests use clock.FreezeTime with baseTime parsed from RFC3339 — use the same approach for new TTL boundary tests to keep them deterministic.

anti_patterns: Using time.Now() inside a Fetcher or anywhere in this package — always use clock.Now()
Adding a mutex around Get without profiling — stampede tolerance is an intentional design choice
Constructing with a nil fetcher — the constructor rejects it but upstream callers should validate early
Using context.Background() in tests when t.Context() is available

decisions.decision: Wrap hashicorp/golang-lru with CacheItemWithTTL instead of using lru's built-in expiry
decisions.rationale: lru's native eviction does not integrate with pkg/clock, making TTL boundary tests non-deterministic. Storing ExpiresAt on the item and checking via clock.Now() gives full test control.

code_examples.scenario: Create a cache that fetches string values by string key with a 10-second TTL

pkg/otelx.purpose: Minimal OpenTelemetry span-context serialization helpers for cross-process propagation over non-HTTP transports (e.g. Kafka message headers). Serializes trace.SpanContext to/from base64-encoded JSON using the OTelSpanContextKey constant.

patterns.name: OTelSpanContextKey constant as the canonical header/attribute name
patterns.description: Always use the OTelSpanContextKey constant ("otel.span.context") when storing or reading the serialized span context. Never hardcode the string.

patterns.name: Base64-over-JSON encoding for transport-agnostic propagation
patterns.description: SpanContext is marshalled to JSON via trace.SpanContext.MarshalJSON() then base64-encoded. Reversal uses base64.DecodeString then json.Unmarshal into trace.SpanContextConfig then trace.NewSpanContext.

patterns.name: Nil-check DeserializeSpanContext return value
patterns.description: DeserializeSpanContext returns *trace.SpanContext. Callers must nil-check before dereferencing even when err == nil.
patterns.example: sc, err := otelx.DeserializeSpanContext(headerVal); if err != nil || sc == nil { return }; span := sc.TraceID()

key_file_guides.role: Only file; exports SerializeSpanContext, DeserializeSpanContext, and OTelSpanContextKey constant.
key_file_guides.watch_for: DeserializeSpanContext returns *trace.SpanContext — callers must nil-check. It does not validate the resulting SpanContext (IsValid()); add that check at the call site if needed.

anti_patterns: Storing the serialized span context under any key other than OTelSpanContextKey
Using W3C TraceContext or B3 propagators here — this package uses its own JSON/base64 scheme for non-HTTP transports
Dereferencing the *trace.SpanContext return from DeserializeSpanContext without a nil check

decisions.decision: Custom base64+JSON serialization instead of standard W3C propagator
decisions.rationale: Kafka message headers carry opaque byte slices; the W3C propagator expects an HTTP carrier interface. Base64-over-JSON is self-contained, transport-agnostic, and avoids pulling the full otel propagation API into Kafka message handling.
